Its Washington Avenue corridor is lined with acclaimed restaurants and bars, and its elegant brownstone blocks attract a diverse mix of long-time residents and newcomers.

properties.name: Prospect Lefferts Gardens
properties.summary: Prospect Lefferts Gardens is a historic neighborhood on the southeastern edge of Prospect Park, known for its exceptional concentration of Flemish Revival and neo-Tudor rowhouses built in the early twentieth century. It has a rich Caribbean cultural heritage and a close-knit community that takes pride in preserving its architectural character.

properties.name: Prospect Park
properties.summary: Prospect Park is Brooklyn's premier green space, a 585-acre masterpiece designed by Frederick Law Olmsted and Calvert Vaux that features a large lake, meadows, forests, a bandshell, and a zoo. It serves as the heart and gathering place for a wide swath of Brooklyn's communities year-round.

properties.name: Prospect Park South
properties.summary: Prospect Park South is an extraordinary residential enclave in central Brooklyn developed in the 1890s as a suburban retreat, featuring unusually large freestanding Victorian houses with deep setbacks and lush landscaping that feel unlike almost anywhere else in the city. The neighborhood is a designated historic district that retains much of its original turn-of-the-century grandeur.

properties.name: Queens Village
properties.summary: Queens Village is a large, predominantly residential neighborhood in eastern Queens with a history stretching back to colonial times, when it was known as Little Plains. Today it is a diverse, middle-class community of single-family homes and small apartment buildings, well connected to Manhattan via the Long Island Rail Road.

properties.name: Randall Manor
properties.summary: Randall Manor is a small, residential neighborhood on Staten Island's North Shore between West New Brighton and Snug Harbor, known for its modest homes and quiet streets. It sits close to the scenic waterfront and cultural amenities of Snug Harbor Cultural Center, giving residents easy access to one of the borough's premier green spaces.

properties.name: Randals-Wards Island
properties.summary: Randalls and Wards Islands sit in the East River between Manhattan, the Bronx, and Queens, serving primarily as home to public parkland, athletic fields, and several city social service facilities. The islands are connected to Manhattan by the Triborough Bridge and a pedestrian footbridge at 103rd Street, and their expansive fields and waterfront paths make them a popular destination for sports and recreation.

properties.name: Red Hook
properties.summary: Red Hook is a peninsula on the southwestern waterfront of Brooklyn, isolated from the rest of the borough by the BQE and accessible only by bus or ferry, which has helped preserve its industrial, rough-hewn character. Its converted warehouses, working docks, and waterfront views of the Statue of Liberty and Manhattan give it a uniquely gritty and creative atmosphere.

properties.name: Rego Park
properties.summary: Rego Park is a densely populated, transit-rich neighborhood in central Queens known for its large Jewish and Russian-speaking immigrant community and a thriving retail corridor anchored by the Queens Center Mall. Its name derives from the Real Good Construction Company that built the area's characteristic brick apartment buildings in the 1930s.

properties.name: Richmond Hill
properties.summary: Richmond Hill is a diverse, working-class neighborhood in southwestern Queens with a sizable Guyanese and Indo-Caribbean community, one of the largest such concentrations in the United States. Its commercial heart along Liberty Avenue buzzes with Caribbean restaurants, grocery stores, and cultural institutions, set against a backdrop of Victorian-era row houses.

properties.name: Richmond Town
properties.summary: Richmond Town is home to Historic Richmond Town, a living history village and museum complex that preserves more than 25 historic structures dating back to the 17th century, making it one of New York City's most significant heritage sites. Nestled in the center of Staten Island near La Tourette Park, the area is defined by its deep historical roots and proximity to expansive green space.

properties.name: Richmond Valley
properties.summary: Richmond Valley is one of Staten Island's southernmost neighborhoods, a low-density area bordering the Arthur Kill with a largely rural character defined by open land and scattered residential development. It sits adjacent to the Staten Island Greenbelt's southern reaches and the Charleston neighborhood, contributing to the quiet, outer-borough feel of the island's southern end.

properties.name: Ridgewood
properties.summary: Ridgewood straddles the Queens-Brooklyn border and is celebrated for its remarkably intact rows of yellow-brick Romanesque Revival attached homes, many of which were built in the early 20th century for German immigrant workers. In recent years the neighborhood has attracted artists and young residents drawn by its architecture, lively restaurant scene, and strong community character.

properties.name: Rikers Island
properties.summary: Rikers Island is a 413-acre island in the East River, accessible only by a single bridge from Queens, and home to New York City's main jail complex. It has been the center of ongoing debate and reform efforts, with the city committed to closing the facility and replacing it with smaller borough-based jails.

properties.name: Riverdale
properties.summary: Riverdale is one of the most affluent neighborhoods in the Bronx, perched on wooded hills overlooking the Hudson River near the northern tip of Manhattan. It features grand estates, mid-rise cooperatives, and a strong Jewish community, with a distinctly suburban character rare in New York City.

properties.name: Rochdale
properties.summary: Rochdale Village is a large cooperative housing development in southeastern Queens, built in the 1960s as one of the largest housing cooperatives in the country. The complex houses thousands of residents in mid-rise towers set around a shopping center and green spaces, and has long served as a middle-class anchor for the Jamaica area.

properties.name: Rockaway Beach
properties.summary: Rockaway Beach is a vibrant oceanfront neighborhood on the Rockaway Peninsula in Queens, famous for its wide Atlantic Ocean beach that draws surfers and sunbathers from across the city each summer. The neighborhood has a lively, seasonal energy with beachside bars and bungalows, and has seen significant community rebuilding since Hurricane Sandy in 2012.

properties.name: Rockaway Park
properties.summary: Rockaway Park is a residential beachfront neighborhood on the western end of the Rockaway Peninsula, offering a quieter, more suburban alternative to the busier Rockaway Beach strip nearby. It features single-family bungalows, close-knit blocks, and easy access to both the ocean and Jamaica Bay, with a year-round community that has weathered decades of coastal challenges.

properties.name: Roosevelt Island
properties.summary: Roosevelt Island is a narrow sliver of land in the East River between Manhattan and Queens, accessible via a scenic aerial tramway, the F train, or a bridge from Queens. Originally the site of hospitals and a penitentiary, it was redeveloped in the 1970s as a planned residential community with a car-free main street, a quieter pace of life, and sweeping views of the Manhattan skyline.

properties.name: Rosebank
properties.summary: Rosebank is a diverse, waterfront neighborhood on Staten Island's northeastern shore, situated along the Narrows just south of Tompkinsville and offering sweeping views of Brooklyn and the Verrazano-Narrows Bridge. It is the birthplace of painter Arthur Dove and home to the Garibaldi-Meucci Museum, reflecting both artistic and Italian-American heritage.

properties.name: Rosedale
properties.summary: Rosedale is a residential neighborhood at the far southeastern edge of Queens, bordering Nassau County, with a peaceful suburban feel and largely single-family homes. It has a predominantly African American and Caribbean American population and sits near the Gateway National Recreation Area wetlands along the Jamaica Bay shoreline.

properties.name: Rossville
properties.summary: Rossville is a sparsely populated neighborhood on Staten Island's western shore along the Arthur Kill, known for the eerie Rossville Wrecking Yard (also called the Arthur Kill Ship Graveyard), where dozens of decommissioned vessels slowly rust in the tidal marsh. The area is predominantly residential and industrial, retaining a secluded, edge-of-the-city atmosphere.

properties.name: Roxbury
properties.summary: Roxbury is a small, quiet beachfront community on the Rockaway Peninsula in Queens, situated between Rockaway Beach and the Belle Harbor neighborhood. Like much of the Rockaways, it is defined by its proximity to the Atlantic Ocean and has a tight-knit, year-round residential character alongside seasonal beach activity.

properties.name: Sheepshead Bay
properties.summary: Sheepshead Bay is a seaside neighborhood in southern Brooklyn centered on a sheltered inlet where fishing boats, yachts, and party boats have docked for generations. Its waterfront esplanade is lined with seafood restaurants, and the surrounding blocks reflect a diverse mix of Eastern European, Russian, and Middle Eastern communities.

properties.name: Shore Acres
properties.summary: Shore Acres is a small, upscale residential neighborhood on Staten Island's northeastern tip, perched above the Narrows with grand views of the water and the Verrazano-Narrows Bridge. It is characterized by large, well-maintained homes and a secluded feel, making it one of the more exclusive enclaves on the North Shore.

properties.name: Silver Lake
properties.summary: Silver Lake is a residential neighborhood in northeastern Staten Island centered around Silver Lake Park, a 191-acre green space that was once home to a reservoir serving New York City's water supply. The park's forested paths and golf course give the neighborhood a tranquil, wooded character just minutes from the Staten Island Ferry terminal.

properties.name: SoHo
properties.summary: SoHo, South of Houston Street, is one of Manhattan's most recognized neighborhoods, celebrated for its magnificent concentration of cast-iron architecture and its transformation from an artists' enclave into a premier destination for high-end shopping and dining. Its wide, cobblestoned streets are lined with galleries, flagship stores, and loft-style residences that draw visitors from around the world.

properties.name: Soundview
properties.summary: Soundview is a working-class neighborhood in the southeastern Bronx, anchored by the largely residential streets around Soundview Avenue and home to a predominantly African American and Latino population. The neighborhood includes Soundview Park, which stretches along the Bronx River and offers waterfront greenspace to residents.

properties.name: South Beach
properties.summary: South Beach is a beachfront neighborhood on Staten Island's eastern shore, anchored by the Franklin D. Roosevelt Boardwalk and Beach, one of the longest free public boardwalks in the United States. Once a popular resort destination in the early 20th century, it retains a casual, seaside character with a mix of residential streets and summer activity along the waterfront.

properties.name: South Bronx
properties.summary: The South Bronx encompasses the southernmost sections of the borough, a historically underserved area that became a symbol of urban decline in the 1970s before undergoing decades of rebuilding and reinvestment. Today it is a dynamic, majority Latino community with deep cultural roots in hip-hop and a rapidly evolving arts and development scene.

properties.name: South Ozone Park
properties.summary: South Ozone Park is a dense, multicultural neighborhood in southwestern Queens, home to one of New York City's largest Guyanese communities as well as significant Trinidadian and other Caribbean populations. Located just north of JFK Airport, it features a lively Liberty Avenue commercial corridor and modest attached homes on compact residential streets.

properties.name: Springfield Gardens
properties.summary: Springfield Gardens is a residential neighborhood in southeastern Queens known for its single-family homes and a predominantly African American and Caribbean American community. The neighborhood borders JFK Airport and offers a quieter, more suburban character compared to much of Queens, with Idlewild Park nearby providing green space along the waterfront.

properties.name: Spuyten Duyvil
properties.summary: Spuyten Duyvil is a small, secluded neighborhood at the southwestern tip of the Bronx, wedged between the Hudson River and the Harlem River Ship Canal directly across from Manhattan's Inwood. It has a quiet, almost village-like character with steep, wooded terrain, modest apartment buildings, and sweeping river views.

properties.name: St. Albans
properties.summary: St. Albans is a historic African American neighborhood in southeastern Queens with a proud legacy as home to jazz legends including Count Basie, Ella Fitzgerald, and Lena Horne, who settled here during the mid-20th century. Today it remains a predominantly Black middle-class community of well-maintained one- and two-family homes with a strong sense of cultural heritage.

properties.name: St. George
properties.summary: St. George is the civic heart of Staten Island, anchoring the northeastern waterfront where the Staten Island Ferry Terminal, the Staten Island Railway, and the borough's main government buildings all converge. Named for developer George Law, it has undergone a cultural renaissance in recent years, with Empire Outlets, the St. George Theatre, and new residential development revitalizing its Victorian-era streetscape.

properties.name: Stapleton
properties.summary: Stapleton is a historically significant neighborhood on Staten Island's North Shore, once home to a major U.S. Navy homeport and now undergoing a wave of revitalization with new waterfront development, restaurants, and arts spaces. Its mix of Victorian row houses, a diverse population, and growing creative scene make it one of the most dynamic communities on the island.

properties.name: Stuyvesant Town
properties.summary: Stuyvesant Town is a large planned residential complex on the east side of Manhattan between 14th and 23rd Streets, built in the late 1940s as middle-class housing and still home to tens of thousands of residents. Its distinctive red-brick towers set within landscaped grounds create an insular, community-oriented atmosphere that stands apart from the surrounding grid of the city.

properties.name: Sunnyside
properties.summary: Sunnyside is a hillside residential neighborhood in central Staten Island, offering a quiet, suburban atmosphere with views toward the island's interior. It borders the Staten Island Greenbelt and sits near major roadways, giving it a well-connected yet peaceful character typical of mid-island communities.

properties.name: Sunnyside
properties.summary: Sunnyside is a diverse, close-knit neighborhood in western Queens with excellent transit access to Midtown Manhattan via the 7 train and the LIRR. It is known for the landmarked Sunnyside Gardens, a planned residential development from the 1920s featuring communal green spaces, and for its mix of Irish, Romanian, Korean, and Latin American communities.

properties.name: Sunset Park
properties.summary: Sunset Park is a densely populated, working-class neighborhood on the southwestern slope of Brooklyn overlooking New York Harbor, with one of the city's largest and most vibrant Chinese communities centered on Eighth Avenue alongside a historic Latino community along Fifth Avenue. Its hillside park offers sweeping panoramic views of Manhattan and the harbor, and its waterfront Industry City complex has become a major hub for arts, manufacturing, and food businesses.

properties.name: Sutton Place
properties.summary: Sutton Place is a refined and tranquil enclave on Manhattan's East Side along the East River just south of the Queensboro Bridge, known for its stately prewar apartment buildings, historic townhouses, and a private riverside esplanade. Long one of the city's most exclusive addresses, it has housed diplomats, dignitaries, and Manhattan's elite, with a portion of the neighborhood listed on the National Register of Historic Places.

properties.name: Throggs Neck
properties.summary: Throggs Neck is a peninsula neighborhood in the southeastern Bronx, jutting into the confluence of the East River and Long Island Sound near the Throggs Neck Bridge. It has a close-knit, predominantly Italian American and Irish American community, with a mix of private homes, marinas, and waterfront parks.

properties.name: Todt Hill
properties.summary: Todt Hill is one of the most affluent neighborhoods in all of New York City, occupying the highest natural point on the Eastern Seaboard south of Maine — a 409-foot summit in the center of Staten Island. Its winding roads are lined with grand estates and mansions, and its exclusivity and elevation have long made it a symbol of prestige on the island.

properties.name: Tompkinsville
properties.summary: Tompkinsville is one of the first neighborhoods visitors encounter after arriving by Staten Island Ferry, a dense, diverse community on the northeastern shore with strong Sri Lankan and South Asian immigrant communities. Its main commercial strips buzz with ethnic restaurants and shops, giving it an energetic, multicultural character unlike anywhere else on the island.

properties.name: Tottenville
properties.summary: Tottenville sits at the southernmost tip of Staten Island — and of New York City itself — where the Arthur Kill meets Raritan Bay, giving it a distinctive maritime edge and a sense of geographic finality. This quiet, largely residential neighborhood retains a small-town character, with historic homes, a modest Main Street, and the Conference House, site of a pivotal 1776 peace meeting during the Revolutionary War.

properties.name: Tremont
properties.summary: Tremont is a densely populated neighborhood in the central Bronx, centered along Third Avenue and home to a large and established Puerto Rican and Dominican community. It sits between Crotona Park and Claremont Village, offering residents green space amid a lively mix of bodegas, restaurants, and local businesses.

properties.name: Tribeca
properties.summary: Tribeca, the Triangle Below Canal Street, is an affluent downtown Manhattan neighborhood known for its dramatic 19th-century warehouse buildings converted into luxury lofts, upscale restaurants, and a notable concentration of celebrity residents. Once a commercial and industrial district, it hosts the annual Tribeca Film Festival and has one of the highest real estate values in the city.

properties.name: Two Bridges
properties.summary: Two Bridges is a small, historically immigrant neighborhood on the Lower East Side of Manhattan, named for the Manhattan and Brooklyn Bridges that anchor its northern and southern ends along the East River waterfront. Long home to Chinese and Latino communities, the area has seen significant new high-rise residential development in recent years while retaining its working-class roots.

properties.name: Unionport
properties.summary: Unionport is a small sub-neighborhood tucked within Castle Hill in the southeastern Bronx, situated north of Lafayette Avenue between Westchester Creek and White Plains Road. A working-class residential community, it developed in the early 20th century as immigrants moved outward from Lower Manhattan seeking more space.

properties.name: University Heights
properties.summary: University Heights is a hillside neighborhood in the west-central Bronx, named for the several colleges and universities that have historically called the area home, including New York University's former Bronx campus. Today it is a densely residential, predominantly Latino community with strong commercial corridors along University Avenue and Jerome Avenue.

properties.name: Upper East Side
properties.summary: The Upper East Side is one of Manhattan's most prestigious and well-heeled neighborhoods, stretching along the eastern edge of Central Park and home to Museum Mile, which includes the Metropolitan Museum of Art, the Guggenheim, and numerous other cultural institutions. Its tree-lined streets of prewar apartment buildings and elegant townhouses are synonymous with old New York wealth and sophistication.

properties.name: Upper West Side
properties.summary: The Upper West Side is a culturally rich and intellectual residential neighborhood along the western edge of Central Park, home to Lincoln Center, the American Museum of Natural History, and a wealth of beloved independent bookstores, restaurants, and jazz venues. Its grand prewar apartment buildings and brownstone side streets give it a classic New York ambiance that has long attracted writers, musicians, and academics.

properties.name: Utopia
properties.summary: Utopia is a small, peaceful residential neighborhood in north-central Queens, bordered by Fresh Meadows, Hillcrest, and Jamaica Hills, known for its streets of modest one- and two-family homes. The evocative name dates to a 19th-century utopian community experiment attempted on the site before it was developed into the neighborhood it is today.

properties.name: Van Cortlandt Park
properties.summary: Van Cortlandt Park is a sprawling 1,146-acre park in the northwestern Bronx, one of the largest in the city, featuring ancient forests, freshwater wetlands, the nation's oldest public golf course, and a popular cross-country running trail. The historic Van Cortlandt House Museum, a colonial-era mansion, sits at its heart and draws visitors year-round.

properties.name: Van Nest
properties.summary: Van Nest is a small, tight-knit residential neighborhood in the central Bronx, bounded by the Bronx River Parkway and known for its mix of Italian American heritage and a growing Latino population. The area features predominantly low-rise homes and a quiet, neighborhood feel anchored by local shops along Morris Park Avenue.

properties.name: Vinegar Hill
properties.summary: Vinegar Hill is one of Brooklyn's smallest and most hidden neighborhoods, a tiny enclave of Federal and Greek Revival row houses tucked between DUMBO and the Navy Yard that has the feel of a nineteenth-century village. Its cobblestone streets and preserved historic homes make it one of the most atmospheric corners of the borough.

properties.name: Wakefield
properties.summary: Wakefield is the northernmost neighborhood in the Bronx, sharing a border with Mount Vernon, New York, and defined by its working-class residential character and strong West Indian and African American community. Its main commercial strip along White Plains Road offers a lively mix of Caribbean bakeries, barbershops, and local retailers.

properties.name: Washington Heights
properties.summary: Washington Heights occupies the northern reaches of Manhattan above 155th Street, rising steeply in elevation toward the cliffs of the Hudson, and is one of the city's most vibrant Latino communities — particularly Dominican-American. The neighborhood is home to the Cloisters museum of medieval art, Fort Tryon Park, and a dense, energetic street life that gives it a character all its own.

properties.name: West Brighton
properties.summary: West Brighton is a diverse North Shore Staten Island neighborhood with deep roots going back to Dutch and English colonists, later shaped by waves of Irish, Italian, African American, and Hispanic communities. Centered on Forest Avenue's commercial strip, the neighborhood has seen declining crime and increasing investment since the late 1990s.

properties.name: West Farms
properties.summary: West Farms is a small, densely packed neighborhood in the south-central Bronx, centered around the West Farms Square transit hub where several subway lines converge. Once a 19th-century village along the Bronx River, it is now a largely residential area with a predominantly Latino population and close proximity to the Bronx Zoo and New York Botanical Garden.

properties.name: West Harlem
properties.summary: Manhattanville is a West Harlem neighborhood centered on the former Manhattanville campus, now being transformed by Columbia University's major expansion. Nestled between Morningside Heights and Hamilton Heights, it has a long history as an industrial and transit hub along the Hudson River.

properties.name: West Jamaica Bay Islands
properties.summary: The West Jamaica Bay Islands are a collection of mostly uninhabited marshy islands and mudflats in the western portion of Jamaica Bay, managed as part of the Gateway National Recreation Area. They serve primarily as important habitat for migratory shorebirds and nesting waterbirds rather than as a human community.

properties.name: West Village
properties.summary: The West Village is a charming, intimate corner of Greenwich Village known for its winding cobblestone streets, Federal and Greek Revival townhouses, and thriving restaurant and bar scene. It has long been a center of LGBTQ+ history and culture, and today ranks among Manhattan's most desirable and picturesque neighborhoods.

properties.name: Westchester Heights
properties.summary: Westchester Heights is an eastern Bronx neighborhood built around one of the borough's oldest settlements — a Dutch outpost called "Ostdoorp" that served as the governmental seat of the Bronx and Westchester region until 1756. Today it is defined by a major cluster of hospitals including Jacobi, Einstein, and Bronx State, surrounded by prewar residential streets.

properties.name: Westerleigh
properties.summary: Westerleigh is a quiet, residential neighborhood in central Staten Island historically founded as a temperance community in the late 19th century, giving it an unusually planned and civic-minded origin. Today it is a stable, middle-class enclave with well-kept homes and tree-shaded streets, retaining its peaceful identity far from the bustle of the North Shore.

properties.name: Whitestone
properties.summary: Whitestone is an affluent, waterfront neighborhood in the northeastern corner of Queens, overlooking the Long Island Sound and anchored by the graceful Whitestone Bridge. It is one of Queens' most desirable residential enclaves, known for large private homes, well-tended yards, and a quiet, suburban ambiance far removed from the density of the inner borough.

properties.name: Williamsbridge
properties.summary: Williamsbridge is a residential neighborhood in the northeastern Bronx, developed in the late 19th and early 20th centuries and now home to a diverse mix of African American, Caribbean, and Latino families. The neighborhood is characterized by its tree-lined blocks, modest single-family homes, and the Williamsbridge Oval, a beloved local park built on the site of a former reservoir.

properties.name: Williamsburg
properties.summary: Williamsburg is a dynamic waterfront neighborhood in northern Brooklyn that transformed over the early 2000s from a largely Latino and Hasidic Jewish community into one of the most prominent destinations for nightlife, dining, art, and culture in New York City. Its main drags along Bedford Avenue and the waterfront are packed with boutiques, bars, and restaurants, while its southern half retains a large and insular Satmar Hasidic community.

properties.name: Willowbrook
properties.summary: Willowbrook is a suburban neighborhood in central Staten Island most widely known for the former Willowbrook State School, an institution whose documented abuses of residents with intellectual disabilities in the 1970s led to landmark federal disability rights legislation. Today the site is home to the College of Staten Island, and the surrounding area is a tranquil residential community near the Staten Island Greenbelt.

properties.name: Windsor Terrace
properties.summary: Windsor Terrace is a small, close-knit residential neighborhood wedged between Prospect Park and Greenwood Cemetery, known for its quiet streets, mix of brick row houses and frame buildings, and a strong sense of local identity. It has historically been an Irish-American working-class community and has seen gradual gentrification while retaining its unpretentious neighborhood character.

properties.name: Wingate
properties.summary: Wingate is a small residential neighborhood in central Brooklyn between East Flatbush and Flatbush, home to a predominantly Black community with roots in the Caribbean, particularly from Haiti and Jamaica. Its streets are lined with modest attached homes and two-family houses, and it is served by the large Wingate Park athletic complex.

properties.name: Woodhaven
properties.summary: Woodhaven is a working-class neighborhood in southwestern Queens with a long history as one of the borough's original streetcar suburbs, known for its intact Victorian and early 20th-century housing stock. It borders Forest Park, a large glacial ridge woodland that provides residents with hiking trails and green space, and has a diverse, multi-ethnic population today.

properties.name: Woodlawn
properties.summary: Woodlawn, sometimes called Woodlawn Heights, is a neighborhood at the northern edge of the Bronx with a strong Irish American identity, home to one of the largest Irish immigrant communities in New York City. It is bordered by the historic Woodlawn Cemetery, a sprawling and architecturally significant burial ground that serves as the final resting place of numerous notable Americans.

properties.name: Woodrow
properties.summary: Woodrow is a rural-feeling neighborhood in southwestern Staten Island, characterized by low-density housing, open land, and a relaxed pace that feels far removed from the urban density of most of New York City. It borders the Arthur Kill wetlands and sits near the Greenbelt's southern edge, making it one of the most pastoral corners of the five boroughs.

properties.name: Woodside
properties.summary: Woodside is a vibrant, transit-connected neighborhood in northwestern Queens with a rich immigrant tapestry that includes large Irish, Filipino, Korean, and Mexican communities. Served by the 7 train and the LIRR, it offers a lively commercial scene along Roosevelt Avenue and 61st Street, with attached homes and apartment buildings lining the residential side streets.
